**Murton** is a village and [civil parish](/wiki/Civil_parish "Civil parish") in [County Durham](/wiki/County_Durham "County Durham"), England, eight miles (13 km) east of the city of [Durham](/wiki/Durham,_England "Durham, England") and six miles (9.7 km) south of [Sunderland](/wiki/Sunderland,_Tyne_and_Wear "Sunderland, Tyne and Wear"). It had a population of 4,534,[[1]](#cite_note-NOS-1) which rose to 7,676 at the 2011 Census.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

It was originally a rural agricultural hamlet called Morton,[[3]](#cite_note-3) but the discovery of coal beneath its fields in the 19th century transformed it into an industrial community.[[4]](#cite_note-4) 'Morton became known as Murton Colliery or Murton-in-the-Whins[[5]](#cite_note-whins-5) following the sinking of the pit in 1838 by South Hetton Coal Company,[[6]](#cite_note-pitsink-6) and the village was a productive [coal mining](/wiki/Coal_mining "Coal mining") community for more than a century. The pit employed more than 1000 men at its peak and featured in a *[Picture Post](/wiki/Picture_Post "Picture Post")* article showing the 'vesting' of the mine at nationalisation in 1947.[[7]](#cite_note-picpost-7)

The village also had the South East Durham Cooperative Bakery and a Northern Bus Company garage as added sources of jobs. In 1955 a by-product works for coal was established for the production mainly of [coke](/wiki/Coke_(fuel) "Coke (fuel)"). The [mine](/wiki/Mining "Mining") and other employment opportunities closed in the 1990s along with many other [County Durham](/wiki/County_Durham "County Durham") mines and now the old spoil heaps are covered by a retail outlet development [Dalton Park](/wiki/Dalton_Park "Dalton Park"), bringing much-needed new employment.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Murton,_County_Durham&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Further information: [History of County Durham](/wiki/History_of_County_Durham "History of County Durham")

The discovery of coal beneath the fields of East [Durham](/wiki/Durham,_England "Durham, England") during the 19th century transformed the tiny hamlet of Morton into the thriving township of Murton.

Decades later, however, the decision to close Murton's successful colliery almost, but not quite, succeeded in turning the close-knit community into a ghost town. Millions of pounds in [Government](/wiki/Government "Government") and private investment is now being pumped into Murton, changing the face of the former [pit village](/wiki/Pit_village "Pit village") almost daily.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/John_Wilson_Carmichael_-_A_View_of_Murton_Colliery_near_Seaham%2C_County_Durham_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg/250px-John_Wilson_Carmichael_-_A_View_of_Murton_Colliery_near_Seaham%2C_County_Durham_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_Wilson_Carmichael_-_A_View_of_Murton_Colliery_near_Seaham,_County_Durham_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)

John Wilson Carmichael *A View of Murton Colliery near Seaham, County Durham*, 1843

Fewer than 100 people lived in the village of Murton before 1830 but following the sinking of the mine in 1838 the population had grown to 1,387 by 1851. [Miners](/wiki/Miner "Miner") flocked to Murton from across [County Durham](/wiki/County_Durham "County Durham") and [Northumberland](/wiki/Northumberland "Northumberland") in the early years, with people later uprooting from [Devon](/wiki/Devon "Devon"), [Cornwall](/wiki/Cornwall "Cornwall") and [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland") too. Many of the road and place names reflected the original roots of these pitmen, such as the [Cornwall](/wiki/Cornwall "Cornwall") estate, and dozens of different [accents](/wiki/Accent_(dialect) "Accent (dialect)") could be heard in the streets.

Work on the colliery – one of the pioneering mines of the East Durham coalfield — began on 19 February 1838 but it was five years before the first coals were drawn. Problems with pockets of shifting sand and the depth of the magnesian limestone overlying the coal delayed the work, making the project hugely expensive. Late shifts were even introduced for the first time, so that excavation could be carried out around the clock to finish the three-shafted pit as quickly as possible.

Just five years after the colliery opened, however, there was an explosion on 15 August 1848, near the Polka East shaft, which killed 16 miners. The tragedy left villagers shaken, but the village itself continued to flourish. Indeed, by 1856 Murton was almost unrecognisable from the [hamlet](/wiki/Hamlet_(place) "Hamlet (place)") it had once been. Scores of terraced houses had been built to house the miners and the village now boasted three pubs and a new school, plus gas and coke works. As prospective miners continued to flood in so the number of tradesmen grew, with Murton Colliery Co-operative Society helping to serve the village by 1890.

As the village flourished, so too did the colliery. It was modernised after [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") and jn 1922 a Koepe friction winding engine was installed in the West Pit. New pithead baths followed in 1939, described as being "of especially pleasant design”, and *Murton'*s swimming pool was opened in 1961.

But despite high productivity and a loyal workforce the decision was taken to close the pit in 1991. Campaigners fought against the plans but failed to stop them. The Koepe winding engine was transferred to the [Bowes Railway](/wiki/Bowes_Railway "Bowes Railway") following the controversial closure and in 1994 the colliery's winding tower was demolished. Murton's once-thriving pit community was now no more.

* 1851: Population 1,395

* 1892: Murton toll gates removed
* 1902: Three putters killed in pit, two aged 14, one aged 18
* 1906: [Colliery](/wiki/Colliery "Colliery") draws 4,131 tons of coal in one day
* 1910: [Miners](/wiki/Miners "Miners") strike over Eight Hours Bill
* 1913: Foundation stone laid for first council house
* 1914: First [public telephone](/wiki/Payphone "Payphone") for Murton, based in post office
* 1922: [Cenotaph](/wiki/Cenotaph "Cenotaph") unveiled
* 1923: Coal drawn from West Pit for first time
* 1927: Bus service to [Sunderland](/wiki/Sunderland,_Tyne_and_Wear "Sunderland, Tyne and Wear") introduced by Northern buses
* 1930: First talking pictures at Murton Empire
* 1931: Flush toilets installed in colliery houses
* 1935: Miners started carrying electric lamps
* 1939: Pit-head baths opened
* 1950: [X-ray](/wiki/X-ray "X-ray") van visited Murton for the first time
* 1953: The first paid annual holiday leave of two weeks was introduced
* 1957: A new [library](/wiki/Library "Library") was opened in Barnes Road
* 1958: Murton Brickworks closed
* 1961: Murton's swimming pool was opened
* 1982: First memorial service held to commemorate fatalities at Murton pit
* 1990: Miners fought to keep the pit open
* 1991: *Murton Colliery* closed
* 1994: Pit winding tower demolished despite being listed
* 2003: Dalton Park opening date
* 2023: One of the filming locations for the film directed by [Ken Loach](/wiki/Ken_Loach "Ken Loach") [The Old Oak](/wiki/The_Old_Oak "The Old Oak") about a previously thriving mining community[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## Governance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Murton,_County_Durham&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Governance")]

An [electoral ward](/wiki/Wards_and_electoral_divisions_of_the_United_Kingdom "Wards and electoral divisions of the United Kingdom") of the same name exists. It includes [Dalton-le-Dale](/wiki/Dalton-le-Dale "Dalton-le-Dale") and surrounding areas and had a total population at the 2011 Census of 7,975.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Like the rest of the United Kingdom, Murton has a [temperate](/wiki/Temperate "Temperate") climate. At 643.3 millimetres (25 in)[[14]](#cite_note-DH_Climate-14) the average annual rainfall is lower than the national average of 1,125 millimetres (44 in).[[15]](#cite_note-UK_Climate-15) Equally there are only around 121.3 days[[14]](#cite_note-DH_Climate-14) where more than 1 millimetre (0.04 in) of rain falls compared with a national average of 154.4 days.[[15]](#cite_note-UK_Climate-15) The area sees on average 1374.6 hours of sunshine per year,[[14]](#cite_note-DH_Climate-14) compared with a national average of 1125.0 hours.[[15]](#cite_note-UK_Climate-15) There is [frost](/wiki/Frost_(temperature) "Frost (temperature)") on 52 days[[14]](#cite_note-DH_Climate-14) compared with a national average of 55.6 days.[[15]](#cite_note-UK_Climate-15) Average daily maximum and minimum temperatures are 12.5 and 5.2 °C (54.5 and 41.4 °F)[[14]](#cite_note-DH_Climate-14) compared with a national averages of 12.1 and 5.1 °C (53.8 and 41.2 °F) respectively.[[15]](#cite_note-UK_Climate-15)
